questions.question: Explain the difference between logical address and physical address with an example.
keyPoints: Logical address is generated by CPU, physical address is in main memory
Logical address is virtual, physical address is actual
MMU translates between them
Example: CPU generates address 1000, MMU maps to physical address 5000

questions.question: What is memory management? List any three important functions of a memory manager.
keyPoints: Memory management controls allocation/deallocation of memory to processes
Functions: allocation of memory space, protection, sharing, address translation, swapping
Ensures memory protection between processes
Maximizes utilization of available memory

questions.question: Describe the three types of address binding. Which one provides maximum flexibility and why?
keyPoints: Compile-time binding: addresses fixed at compile time
Load-time binding: addresses resolved when program is loaded
Execution-time binding: addresses resolved during execution using hardware (MMU)
Execution-time binding provides maximum flexibility as processes can be relocated during execution

questions.question: What is swapping? How does it help in memory management?
keyPoints: Swapping moves entire processes between main memory and secondary storage
When RAM is full, less active processes are moved to disk (swap out)
Active processes are swapped back to RAM when needed
Enables execution of more processes than physical memory can hold

questions.question: Define internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted memory within allocated partition (fixed-size partitions)
Example: process needs 50KB, gets 64KB partition, 14KB wasted inside
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in small blocks (variable partitioning)
Example: three 10KB gaps exist but 25KB contiguous request cannot be satisfied

questions.question: Explain the purpose of base and limit registers in memory protection.
keyPoints: Base register stores starting physical address of a process
Limit register stores size of process's memory partition
Every logical address is checked: address < limit
If valid, physical address = base + logical address; otherwise trap generated

questions.question: What is dynamic loading and dynamic linking? How do they differ?
keyPoints: Dynamic loading: loads routines into memory only when called (saves memory)
Dynamic linking: links shared libraries at load/execution time (saves memory, allows sharing)
Both occur at runtime, not compile time
Loading brings code into memory; linking connects to library code already in memory

questions.question: Describe the memory hierarchy in a computer system. Explain why different levels of memory are needed and how they interact with each other.
keyPoints: Hierarchy: CPU Registers -> Cache -> Main Memory (RAM) -> SSD -> Magnetic Disk
Each level is faster but smaller than the previous
Registers: fastest, few bytes, inside CPU
Cache: 1-100MB, bridges CPU and RAM speed gap
RAM: 4-64GB, main workspace
Secondary storage: TB scale, persistent but slow
Principle of locality: programs access same data repeatedly, cache keeps frequently used data close
Trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity

questions.question: Explain the working of a Memory Management Unit (MMU). How does it help in implementing virtual memory and memory protection?
keyPoints: MMU is hardware between CPU and physical memory
CPU generates logical addresses, MMU translates to physical addresses
Contains base register (relocation register) for address translation
Every memory access: physical = base + logical
Limit register provides memory protection: if logical >= limit, trap to OS
May include TLB for fast translation caching
Enables virtual memory by allowing non-contiguous physical allocation
Multiple processes can share physical memory while believing they have contiguous address space

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of fixed partitioning and variable partitioning in contiguous memory allocation.
keyPoints: FIXED PARTITIONING: Simple to implement, less overhead; Disadvantages: internal fragmentation, limited process size, limited number of processes
VARIABLE PARTITIONING: No internal fragmentation, better memory utilization; Disadvantages: external fragmentation, complex allocation (requires tracking free holes)
Compaction can solve external fragmentation but causes overhead
Best fit, first fit, worst fit are allocation strategies for variable partitioning
Variable partitioning better for memory efficiency but requires sophisticated management

questions.question: What is virtual memory? Explain how it enables execution of programs larger than physical memory. Discuss its benefits and any associated overhead.
keyPoints: Virtual memory creates illusion of larger contiguous memory space than physically available
Uses disk space as extension of RAM (swap space/pagefile)
Pages/segments not currently needed are stored on disk
When needed, OS loads them back to RAM (may evict others)
BENEFITS: Run programs larger than RAM, more processes in memory, simplified programming
OVERHEAD: Page faults cause disk I/O (slow), context switching may increase,Thrashing if too many page faults
